edge: CNNs are trained to detect infrared small targets in a fully supervised manner.

node_2: label evolution with single point supervision (lesps)
edge: CNNs are trained using the label evolution with single point supervision (LESPS) to progressively expand the point labels.
The label evolution with single point supervision (LESPS) framework helps in obtaining a pixel-level target mask to train CNNs in an end-to-end manner.

IoU and object-level probability of detection are two key metrics in the field of object detection, where IoU measures the overlap between the predicted and ground truth bounding boxes, while object-level probability of detection focuses on accurately detecting objects in an image.
Object-level probability of detection is a key component in various detection methods such as contrast-based and low rank-based methods for SIRST detection.
Detection methods have evolved from handcrafted features to compact deep learning approaches like CNNs for more efficient SIRST detection.
Compact deep learning techniques have been introduced to tackle the challenges in SIRST detection, aiming to improve the performance of detection models.
SIRST detection has seen advancements with the integration of deep learning methods, which have the potential to unlock new exploration opportunities in the field.
Deep learning has been extensively explored for natural images, but there is a need for further research to unlock the full potential of deep learning for SIRST detection.
Deep learning has been extensively explored for natural images

node_2: label evolution with single point supervision (lesps)
edge: The proposed framework, LESPS, leverages the intermediate network predictions in the context of single-point supervision for weakly supervised SIRST detection, indicating a shift towards more advanced labeling techniques.

During the training of SIRST detection networks, CNNs tend to segment pixels near the targets with low confidence initially, gradually learning to predict ground truth point labels with high confidence, implying a relationship between the CNNs and region-to-region mapping.

The 'mapping degeneration' phenomenon observed during the training of SIRST detection networks is attributed to the special imaging mechanism of infrared system, suggesting a causal relationship between the infrared system and mapping degeneration.
LESPS leverages the intermediate network predictions in the training phase to update the current labels, which serve as supervision until the next label update
The network predictions can finally approximate the updated pseudo mask labels through iterative label update and network training
LESPS can significantly reduce the annotation cost and allow the network to achieve pixel-level SIRST detection in an end-to-end manner
Region-to-region mapping represents the mapping learned by CNNs from target regions in images to a cluster of pixels near the targets
Region-to-point mapping represents the mapping from target regions in images to the GT point labels
End-to-end represents achieving point-to-mask label regression and direct pixel-level inference in one training
End-to-end represents achieving point-to-mask label regression and direct pixel-level inference in one training
The mapping degeneration phenomenon is leveraged by LESPS to automatically regress pixel-level pseudo labels from the given point labels

node_2: special imaging mechanism of infrared systems
edge: Mapping degeneration phenomenon is attributed to the special imaging mechanism of infrared systems where only intensity information is available without structure and texture details.

node_2: high local contrast of infrared small targets
edge: The mapping degeneration is associated with the high local contrast of infrared small targets where pixels within the target region have significant brightness differences.
The phenomenon of mapping degeneration is further affected by the easy-to-hard learning property of CNNs as they tend to learn simple mappings first.
Mapping degeneration involves the idea that region-to-region mapping is an intermediate result of region-to-point mapping due to unique characteristics of infrared small targets.
CNNs play a role in contributing to the process of mapping degeneration by extending mapping regions beyond point labels.
Region-to-region mapping is seen as an easier task and thus tends to be an intermediate step in the region-to-point mapping process.
CNNs contribute to the region-to-point mapping process by extending the mapping regions beyond just point labels.
The phenomenon of mapping degeneration is noted to be applicable in various scenes with infrared small targets due to background clutter.

The method described in the context focuses on weakly-supervised SIRST detection using single-point supervision. This highlights the relationship between these two key concepts.
The context mentions the discovery of the mapping degeneration phenomenon and the proposal of a label evolution framework named LELPS, indicating a direct relation between these two concepts.

The label evolution framework, LELPS, is proposed in the context as a solution to achieve point-to-mask regression with single-point supervision, establishing a clear relationship between these two terms.
The approach introduced in the context allows networks to be trained for SIRST detection in an end-to-end manner, linking the concepts of networks and SIRST detection.

The extensive experiments and insightful visualizations mentioned in the context serve to demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the method, showing a relationship between experiments and method effectiveness.

The method described in the context can be applied to different networks to achieve performance levels over 70% and 95% of its fully supervised performance on pixel-level IoU and object-level Pd respectively, indicating the application-to-performance relationship.

The context aims to inspire researchers by presenting interesting findings and promising results that can lead to rethinking the feasibility of achieving state-of-the-art performance in SIRST detection with much weaker supervision, connecting the concepts of researchers and method inspiration.
